model.label: Claude Opus 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Hybrid reasoning model that pushes the frontier for coding and AI agents, featuring a 1M context window.

pick.rationale: Houston's historically elite defense—ranked 4th nationally with an AdjD of 91.0-91.9—is the decisive factor in this classic irresistible force vs immovable object matchup, as Kelvin Sampson's suffocating system is specifically built to neutralize even the nation's best offenses like Illinois's #1-ranked attack (AdjO 131.2). Houston holds a slight but meaningful edge in overall adjusted efficiency margin (+33.55 vs +32.14) driven by their two-way balance, and their 28-5 record reflects significantly more consistency than Illinois's 24-8 mark with eight losses. While Illinois's generational offense led by Kasparas Jakucionis gives them a legitimate path to an upset, Houston's defensive identity, Big 12 battle-testing, and tournament pedigree under Sampson should allow the Cougars to control tempo and advance to the Elite Eight.

reasoningStep.title: South Sweet 16 2: Illinois (3) vs Houston (2)
reasoningStep.summary: Houston's elite defense (#4 nationally, AdjD 91.0) should contain Illinois's #1 offense enough to prevail in a tight matchup between two top-7 teams, with Houston holding a slight overall efficiency edge (+33.55 vs +32.14) and a significantly better record (28-5 vs 24-8).
evidence: Houston KenPom #5 (AdjEM +33.55, AdjO 124.6 #16, AdjD 91.0 ~#4); Illinois KenPom #7 (AdjEM +32.14, AdjO 131.2 #1, AdjD 99.1)
Houston Torvik #5 (AdjOE 125.1 #13, AdjDE 91.9 #4); Illinois Torvik #6 (AdjOE 132.0 #2, AdjDE 98.1 #24)

Houston's defense is roughly 8 points per 100 possessions better than Illinois's (91.0 vs 99.1), while Illinois's offense is roughly 6.5 points better than Houston's (131.2 vs 124.6) — Houston's defensive advantage is larger
Houston 28-5 record in Big 12 vs Illinois 24-8 in Big Ten; Houston dealt with mid-season injuries to Emanuel Sharp (foot) but appears healthy for tournament
Chicago Sun-Times article specifically flagged Houston as a ceiling for Illinois's tournament run, noting the difficulty of the Sweet 16 matchup

**Ratings Comparison:**
- **Houston**: KenPom #5 (AdjEM +33.55, AdjO 124.6 #16, AdjD 91.0 #4), Torvik #5 (AdjOE 125.1 #13, AdjDE 91.9 #4)
- **Illinois**: KenPom #7 (AdjEM +32.14, AdjO 131.2 #1, AdjD 99.1), Torvik #6 (AdjOE 132.0 #2, AdjDE 98.1 #24)

**Key Matchup Dynamics:**
- Houston has the nation's #4 defense (AdjD 91.0-91.9) vs Illinois's #1 offense (AdjO 131.2-132.0) — this is the defining clash
- Illinois has a solid but not elite defense (AdjD 98.1-99.1, ~24th) vs Houston's strong offense (AdjO 124.6-125.1, ~13-16th)
- Houston's adjusted efficiency margin is ~1.4 points higher (+33.55 vs +32.14)
- Houston had a mid-season rough patch (3-game losing streak in February) with Emanuel Sharp's foot injury and other health concerns, but appears to have recovered
- Illinois has the nation's most potent offense but Houston's defense is specifically designed to neutralize elite offenses

**Health:**
- Houston dealt with Emanuel Sharp (sprained foot) and hand injuries earlier but both appear to have been playing through
- Illinois appears relatively healthy with Jakucionis and Ivisic available

**Stylistic matchup:**
- This is a classic irresistible force (Illinois #1 offense) vs immovable object (Houston #4 defense) matchup
- Houston's defense historically performs well in tournament settings under Kelvin Sampson
- Illinois's 24-8 record (8 losses) vs Houston's 28-5 record suggests Houston has been more consistent
- Houston is the higher seed (2 vs 3) with a slightly better overall efficiency margin

Houston's elite defense should be the deciding factor, as it's easier for a great defense to slow down a great offense in a single-elimination game than for an offense to simply outscore a suffocating defensive system.
